title: The Problem with Plain SGD
concept: Plain SGD zig-zags inefficiently across steep ravines in the loss landscape, and can get stuck oscillating or stalling on flat plateaus and saddle points — the exact terrain deep networks are full of.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why do deep learning training loops usually update weights using mini-batches instead of the full dataset every step?

options.text: Full-batch updates are too slow and memory-heavy for huge datasets and networks
It has nothing to do with speed or memory — it's just a coding convention
Full-batch updates actually use LESS memory than mini-batches

exercises.explain: Right! Mini-batches keep training fast and GPU-friendly. But even with the right batch size, plain SGD still struggles once it hits the deep network's tricky terrain...

cards.title: A clumsy tightrope walker
cards.body: Drop plain SGD into a narrow, steep-walled ravine in the loss landscape, and instead of marching straight down the middle, it lurches side to side — wasting steps bouncing off the walls.

cards.title: Zig-zagging in ravines
cards.body: When the loss surface is much steeper in one direction than another (a ravine), each SGD step gets dominated by the steep direction, so the path oscillates back and forth across the ravine instead of moving smoothly along it toward the bottom.

cards.title: Skiing a narrow, steep-walled gully
cards.body: Imagine skiing down a narrow gully with steep walls: instead of carving smoothly down the middle, you keep bouncing wall to wall, losing speed and time with every bounce, barely making forward progress.

cards.title: Stuck on the flats
cards.body: On a long flat plateau — barely any slope in any direction — the gradient is tiny, so SGD's steps shrink to a crawl. It can wander for a long time before finding a direction that leads back downhill.

cards.title: Saddle points aren't 'stuck forever,' but they're slow
cards.body: You might think a saddle point completely traps an optimizer like a true minimum would. In reality, plain SGD CAN eventually escape — the flat directions aren't perfectly flat — but it can crawl agonizingly slowly through the flat stretch first.

cards.title: Ravine zig-zag vs. plateau stall
cards.body: Two ways plain SGD wastes steps:

left.text:
Bounces wall to wall
Zig-zags instead of progressing

right.text:
Tiny gradient, tiny steps
Crawls toward a stall

exercises.prompt: What happens when plain SGD encounters a narrow ravine in the loss landscape (steep side walls, gentle slope along the bottom)?

options.text: It zig-zags, oscillating side to side instead of moving smoothly down the ravine toward the bottom
It ignores the ravine and moves in a perfectly straight line
It immediately finds the global minimum

exercises.explain: Right! Each step gets dominated by the steep side-to-side slope, wasting motion instead of progressing along the ravine.

exercises.prompt: True or false: on a flat plateau, the gradient is large, so SGD moves quickly across it.
exercises.explain: False! On a flat plateau the gradient is TINY, so plain SGD's steps shrink and it can crawl for a long stretch before escaping.

exercises.sentence: Plain SGD tends to ___ back and forth across steep-walled ravines instead of moving smoothly down them.
exercises.explain: That side-to-side zigzag wastes steps that could have gone toward making real progress down the ravine.

exercises.prompt: Which training log pattern is a telltale sign of plain SGD struggling in a steep ravine?

options.text: The loss bounces sharply up and down step after step instead of steadily decreasing
The loss decreases smoothly and steadily every single step
The loss instantly drops to zero on the first step

exercises.explain: Right — a bouncing, jagged loss curve is the classic sign of SGD zig-zagging across a steep-walled ravine.

exercises.prompt: You're watching a deep network's training loss curve. For hundreds of steps it barely moves at all — the changes are almost too small to see. What's the most likely explanation, and is the optimizer 'stuck' forever?

options.text: It's likely crawling across a flat plateau where the gradient is tiny; plain SGD isn't permanently stuck, but it can take a very long time to find a direction back downhill
The network has finished training perfectly and reached zero error
This means the loss landscape has no more weights left to adjust
The optimizer is broken and will never move again no matter what

exercises.explain: Exactly! Flat plateaus cause tiny gradients and painfully slow crawling — not a permanent trap, but a real slowdown. Next up: a fix that carries momentum through exactly these kinds of flat, sluggish stretches.
